children.name: Archilochus
children.description: Bee hummingbirds — tiny, iridescent hummingbirds of North and Central America. Archilochus species are among the smallest birds on Earth, weighing just 3-4 grams but undertaking extraordinary migrations across the Gulf of Mexico. Their wings beat up to 80 times per second, producing the characteristic humming sound that gives the family its name.

children.name: Archilochus colubris
children.description: The only hummingbird breeding in eastern North America. Iridescent green above, white below, with a brilliant ruby-red throat in males. Remarkably, it migrates non-stop across the Gulf of Mexico — 800 km in one flight, impossible for any other land bird of its size.

children.name: Archilochus alexandri
children.description: Widespread across western North America. Males have a velvety black chin bordered by an iridescent purple band, visible only in good light. Highly adaptable, found from desert canyons to suburban gardens. Winters in Mexico.

children.name: Calypte
children.description: Small to medium-sized hummingbirds of North America. Anna's Hummingbird is the only hummingbird resident year-round in the Pacific Northwest, surviving cold winters by entering torpor on freezing nights.

children.name: Calypte anna
children.description: A stunning hummingbird of the Pacific coast. Males have a rose-red crown and throat that flash brilliant magenta in sunlight. Unlike most temperate hummingbirds, it does not migrate south; instead, it survives mild Pacific winters by entering nightly torpor. Named after Anna Massena, Duchess of Rivoli.

children.name: Selasphorus
children.description: Small, aggressive hummingbirds of western North America. Rufous Hummingbirds are notoriously territorial, chasing birds many times their size. Despite weighing only 3 grams, they undertake one of the longest migrations of any hummingbird — from Alaska to Mexico.

children.name: Selasphorus rufus
children.description: Brilliant rufous-orange body with an iridescent red throat. Breeds from southeastern Alaska to Oregon, wintering in Mexico. One of the feistiest hummingbirds, known to chase away birds and even squirrels from feeders. Its migration route is over 4,000 km one way.

children.name: Chrysolampis
children.description: The ruby-topaz hummingbird — a monotypic genus of small, compact hummingbirds found from Panama to northern South America. The male's plumage combines brilliant ruby-red and golden topaz colours that shift with the light. They are birds of open and semi-open habitats, often visiting gardens and forest edges.

children.name: Chrysolampis mosquitus
children.description: A dazzling small hummingbird whose male shimmers with a ruby-red crown and nape that transitions to golden topaz on the throat and breast. The female is green above and pale grey below with rufous flanks. Ranging from Panama to Brazil, it is common in open country, gardens, and savannas, where it feeds on a variety of tubular flowers.

children.name: Anthracothorax
children.description: Mango hummingbirds — medium to large hummingbirds found in the Neotropics. Males have velvety black or green plumage with brilliant metallic throats ranging from emerald green to amethyst. They are powerful fliers with broad wings.

children.name: Anthracothorax nigricollis
children.description: A widespread South American hummingbird found from Panama to northern Argentina and Brazil. Glossy green upperparts, white underparts, and a distinctive black throat stripe bordered by iridescent green. Found in open habitats, gardens, and forest edges.

children.name: Anthracothorax prevostii
children.description: A large, elegant mango with a brilliant green throat and breast, a purple-black central belly stripe, and a slightly curved black bill. It ranges from Veracruz, Mexico, through Central America to northern South America. It is a common visitor to flowering trees and gardens, often hovering conspicuously at large tropical blooms.

children.name: Florisuga
children.description: The jacobins — large, robust hummingbirds with striking pied and iridescent plumage. They are named for the hooded pattern of the White-necked Jacobin, which resembles the white cowls of Jacobin monks. These bold hummingbirds are known for dominating feeding territories and for their distinctive, buzzy flight sounds.

children.name: Florisuga mellivora
children.description: A stunning hummingbird with a white head, neck, and belly contrasting with a dark blue hood and bright green back. Ranging from southern Mexico to the Amazon basin, it is one of the most eye-catching hummingbirds of lowland rainforest. Males are highly territorial at flowering trees, producing a loud, buzzing wing sound during aggressive chases.

children.name: Florisuga fusca
children.description: A dark, elegant hummingbird endemic to coastal Brazil's Atlantic Forest, Paraguay, and northeastern Argentina. Its plumage is predominantly dark grey-brown with a white patch on the lower belly and a white crescent on the nape. It inhabits humid forests, plantations, and gardens, often visiting banana flowers and other large tropical blooms.

children.name: Colibri
children.description: The violet-ears — medium to large hummingbirds named for the iridescent violet ear patch that is conspicuous in all species. They are among the most vocal hummingbirds, with complex, chattering songs delivered from exposed perches. Found from Mexico to the high Andes, they are robust and adaptable birds of open montane habitats.

children.name: Colibri delphinae
children.description: A duller, more subtly coloured violet-ear with brownish-olive plumage and a distinctive violet ear patch that flashes in good light. It ranges from Guatemala through the Andes to Bolivia, inhabiting cloud forest edges and clearings. It is less aggressive than its congeners, often foraging lower in the understory.

children.name: Colibri coruscans
children.description: A spectacular, large violet-ear with glittering green plumage, a deep blue belly, and a brilliant violet ear patch. It is common in the high Andes from Venezuela to northwestern Argentina, thriving in puna grassland, cloud forest, and even urban parks. Its loud, persistent song is one of the first bird sounds heard at dawn in Andean settlements.

children.name: Chalybura
children.description: The plumeleteers — medium to large hummingbirds of lowland Neotropical forests. Males are predominantly iridescent green with bright blue or violet undertail coverts that are flared in display. They are powerful, aggressive fliers that dominate the lower forest strata in their range.

children.name: Chalybura buffonii
children.description: A large, dark hummingbird of northern South America, with iridescent green body plumage and a prominent white vent that gives it its name. The male has a violet-blue throat and a deeply forked tail. It inhabits lowland forests, plantations, and gardens from Panama to Venezuela and Peru.

children.name: Thalurania
children.description: The woodnymphs — brilliantly coloured hummingbirds of Neotropical forests. Males have vibrant green bodies with shining blue or violet crowns, throats, and bellies that gleam in the dim forest understory. They are agile, fast-flying birds that dart through the forest interior, visiting scattered flowers on trap-line routes.

children.name: Thalurania colombica
children.description: A glittering hummingbird of lowland forests from Guatemala to Ecuador. The male has an emerald-green body, a violet crown, and a blue-green throat and belly. It is one of the most common hummingbirds in its range, found in both primary and secondary forests where it feeds on a variety of flowering plants.

children.name: Thalurania furcata
children.description: The most widespread woodnymph, found across most of South America east of the Andes. The male has a glowing green body, a violet-blue crown, and a deeply forked blue-black tail. Females are green above and white below. It inhabits the understory and edges of humid lowland forests, visiting small tubular flowers.

children.name: Thalurania glaucopis
children.description: A splendid woodnymph endemic to the Atlantic Forest of Brazil, Paraguay, and northeastern Argentina. The male has a vivid violet cap and throat, golden-green underparts, and a blue-black forked tail. It is a common resident of humid forest and shaded plantations, often seen flitting through the understory in search of nectar.

children.name: Chlorostilbon
children.description: Emerald hummingbirds — a large genus of small, slender hummingbirds found throughout the Americas. Most species are predominantly brilliant emerald green, with the male's throat and crown often showing a different metallic hue. They are among the most active and acrobatic of hummingbirds, with rapid, buzzing wingbeats.

children.name: Chlorostilbon mellisugus
children.description: A tiny, brilliant green hummingbird with a shining blue tail that contrasts vividly with its emerald body. It ranges widely across northern South America, from Colombia to the Guianas and south into Brazil. It prefers open and semi-open habitats, including savannas, gardens, and forest edges.

children.name: Chlorostilbon lucidus
children.description: A beautiful emerald hummingbird with a glittering golden-green belly and a bright blue tail. It is found in central and eastern South America, from Bolivia and central Brazil to Argentina. Common in gardens, parks, and open woodland, it is a regular visitor to urban feeders and flowering shrubs.

children.name: Polytmus
children.description: The goldenthroats — medium-sized hummingbirds of open savanna and scrubland in South America. Their plumage is predominantly green with warm buff or golden underparts and a long, slightly decurved bill. They are characteristic birds of the Guianan and Brazilian savannas, well-camouflaged among the grasses and bushes.

children.name: Polytmus guainumbi
children.description: A graceful, long-billed hummingbird of open savanna, grassland, and scrub in northern and central South America. Its upperparts are bronze-green, the underparts are golden buff, and the tail is white-tipped. It is well adapted to dry habitats and is often seen hovering at low flowers in the cerrado and llanos.

children.name: Lesbia
children.description: The trainbearers — Andean hummingbirds with extraordinarily long, graduated tails that trail behind them in flight like streaming ribbons. The tail of the male can reach up to three times the body length. They inhabit high-altitude scrub and forest edges, where they hover gracefully at flowers with their tails trailing in the breeze.

children.name: Lesbia victoriae
children.description: A spectacular hummingbird of the high Andes from Colombia to Peru, with a male whose black, deeply forked tail extends 20 cm or more — far longer than its body. The body is iridescent green with a black throat and breast. It hovers at flowers in puna scrub and cloud forest edges, its long tail streamers trailing behind.

children.name: Lesbia nuna
children.description: Similar to the Black-tailed Trainbearer but with a green throat and a green-washed tail. It ranges from Colombia to Bolivia at elevations of 2,000 to 4,000 metres in Andean cloud forest and shrubby grassland. The male's tail is less dramatically elongated than its relative but still proportionally longer than the body.

children.name: Aglaeactis
children.description: The sunbeams — medium-sized, high-altitude hummingbirds named for their lustrous, sun-reflecting plumage. They are stout-bodied birds of the high Andes, often found at elevations above 3,000 metres. Their plumage combines rich browns, purples, and iridescent patches that catch the thin mountain light like jewels.

children.name: Aglaeactis aliciae
children.description: A rare and localised sunbeam endemic to a small area of the Peruvian Andes. The male has a brilliant purple back, a rufous belly, and an iridescent golden-green throat. It inhabits high-altitude Polylepis woodland and bushy slopes from 3,000 to 4,200 metres. Classified as Vulnerable due to its restricted range and ongoing habitat loss.

children.name: Aglaeactis cupripennis
children.description: A stunning Andean hummingbird with a coppery-golden back, a violet-blue throat, and a rufous belly. It is found from Colombia to Peru at elevations of 2,500 to 4,000 metres in cloud forest and elfin woodland. Its plumage seems to glow in the low-angle sunlight of the high Andes.

children.name: Coeligena
children.description: The incas and starfrontlets — large, long-billed hummingbirds of Andean cloud forests. Many species have a distinctive white or buff spot behind the eye and a brilliant iridescent throat ornament. They are important pollinators of high-altitude flowering plants, with bills that co-vary with corolla length across the genus.

children.name: Coeligena coeligena
children.description: A relatively plain Coeligena with overall bronze-green plumage, a whitish malar stripe, and a long, slightly decurved black bill. It inhabits humid montane forests from Colombia to Bolivia at elevations of 1,200 to 2,700 metres. Despite its subdued colouration, the metallic bronze sheen on its back is beautiful in good light.

children.name: Coeligena torquata
children.description: A striking Andean hummingbird with a green body, a brilliant blue-green throat, and a distinctive white collar that encircles the neck. It ranges from Venezuela to Bolivia in cloud forests at 1,500 to 3,300 metres. It is one of the most common and recognizable Coeligena species, often visiting flowering shrubs at forest edges.

children.name: Coeligena iris
children.description: One of the most variable and colourful Coeligena species, with a throat that ranges from golden to fiery orange to amethyst depending on the subspecies. The male has a green body, a long straight bill, and a white postocular spot. It is found in the Andes of Ecuador and Peru at elevations of 2,000 to 3,200 metres.

children.name: Coeligena lutetiae
children.description: A large, handsome Coeligena distinguished by its buff-coloured wing patches and glittering blue-green throat. It inhabits high-elevation cloud forests and elfin woodlands in the Andes of Colombia, Ecuador, and northern Peru. It feeds on tubular flowers in the understory and at forest edges, often hovering with its long bill probing deep corollas.

children.name: Coeligena helianthea
children.description: A gorgeous Andean hummingbird with a blue throat, a green crown and back, and a long slightly upturned black bill. It is found in the cloud forests of Colombia and Venezuela, where it frequents flowering trees and shrubs in the mid-story. Its name derives from the Greek 'helios' (sun) and 'anthos' (flower), reflecting its sunlit, flower-rich habitat.

children.name: Opisthoprora
children.description: The mountain avocetbill — a monotypic genus with a uniquely upturned bill unmatched among hummingbirds. The bill curves upward at the tip, an adaptation for feeding on specific corolla shapes in high Andean forests. It is a rare and localised bird of cloud forest edges and stunted elfin woodlands.

children.name: Opisthoprora euryptera
children.description: A unique hummingbird with an unmistakable upturned bill that resembles that of an avocet. It is dull green above and greyish below with a whitish postocular stripe. Endemic to the Andes of Colombia, Ecuador, and Peru at elevations of 2,200 to 3,100 metres, it is rarely seen due to its small population and remote cloud forest habitat.

children.name: Pterophanes
children.description: The great sapphirewing — a monotypic genus of very large, powerful hummingbirds found in the high Andes. It is one of the largest hummingbirds, exceeded only by the Giant Hummingbird. Its brilliant blue wings flash conspicuously in flight against the dark green body.

children.name: Pterophanes cyanopterus
children.description: An enormous hummingbird, second in size only to the Giant Hummingbird, with striking bright blue wings that contrast with its metallic green body and coppery-bronze back. It inhabits cloud forests and elfin woodlands in the Andes from Colombia to Bolivia at elevations of 2,500 to 3,700 metres. Its deep, resonant wing hum is audible from a considerable distance.

children.name: Phaethornis
children.description: Hermit hummingbirds — a large group of tropical hummingbirds with long, decurved bills and elongated central tail feathers. Unlike most hummingbirds, hermits are duller in colour (browns, greys, and greens) and do not gather at leks. They are specialised for feeding on flowers with long, curved corollas, and are important pollinators of tropical plants.

children.name: Phaethornis longirostris
children.description: A large hermit with a strikingly long, decurved bill, found from southern Mexico to Ecuador. Brownish-green above, pale below with a dark mask. Its song is a series of high-pitched notes. Pollinates heliconias and other long-tubed flowers.

children.name: Phaethornis superciliosus
children.description: Found across the Amazon Basin and the Guianas. Distinguished by its long, graduated tail and prominent white supercilium. Feeds by trap-lining — visiting a regular circuit of flowers in the understory of lowland rainforest.

children.name: Patagona
children.description: The giant hummingbirds — the largest hummingbirds in the world, reaching 23 cm in length and weighing up to 24 grams. Despite their name, they are still tiny compared to most birds. Found in the high Andes, they hover like all hummingbirds but with a slower, more deliberate wingbeat.

children.name: Patagona gigas
children.description: The largest hummingbird, measuring up to 23 cm. Found in the Andes from Ecuador to Argentina and Chile. Drab brownish-grey plumage with a rufous belly — surprisingly inconspicuous for such a record-breaker. Its wingbeat is slow enough to be visible, about 15 beats per second.

children.name: Topaza
children.description: Topaz hummingbirds — two of the most spectacular hummingbirds in the Neotropics, found in the Amazon Basin. Males have iridescent crimson, golden, and green plumage that makes them look like living jewels. Their long, crossed tail feathers are unique among birds.

children.name: Topaza pella
children.description: A breathtaking hummingbird of the Amazon and Guianan shields. Males have a glowing crimson body, golden-green throat, and black wings. The long, crossed central tail feathers are unique. Inhabits lowland rainforest near streams and rivers.

children.name: Lampornis
children.description: Mountain-gems — robust, medium-sized hummingbirds of highland forests from Mexico to Panama. Named for their brilliant throat colours that flash like gemstones in the sun. They inhabit cloud forests and high-elevation oak woodlands.

children.name: Lampornis clemenciae
children.description: The largest hummingbird in North America after the Giant Hummingbird. Iridescent green above, grey below, with a brilliant blue throat. Found in pine-oak woodlands of Mexico and the southwestern United States. Its loud, high-pitched song is one of the most complex of any hummingbird.

children.name: Lampornis amethystinus
children.description: Found in highland forests from Mexico to Honduras. Males have a brilliant amethyst-purple throat and a white postocular stripe. Females lack the throat colour. Feeds on flowers in the mid-story and canopy of cloud forests.

children.name: Oreotrochilus
children.description: Hillstars — high-altitude hummingbirds of the Andean páramo, found at elevations up to 5,200 metres. They tolerate extreme cold by entering deep torpor and roosting in sheltered rock crevices. Their stout bodies and short, straight bills are adapted for feeding on high-altitude flowers.

children.name: Oreotrochilus estella
children.description: A high-altitude specialist found in the Andes from Peru to Chile and Argentina. Greyish-brown above, white below with a rufous belly and a brilliant green or blue throat. Survives at altitudes where night temperatures regularly fall below freezing.

children.name: Ensifera
children.description: The sword-billed hummingbird — a monotypic genus with the most extreme bill of any bird. The bill is longer than the body (up to 12 cm) and is used to feed from flowers with long corollas that no other bird can access. The bird cannot preen with its bill and must use its feet.

children.name: Ensifera ensifera
children.description: The only bird with a bill longer than its body. The sword-like bill reaches 12 cm, while the body is only 10-12 cm. Found in the Andes from Venezuela to Bolivia. Co-evolved with passionflowers and other deep-tubed blooms. When perched, the bird holds its bill at a 45-degree angle to keep its balance.

children.name: Aglaiocercus
children.description: Sylphs — hummingbirds with extraordinarily long, forked tails that can reach twice the body length. The males' tails are adorned with iridescent colours and are used in spectacular aerial courtship displays.

children.name: Aglaiocercus kingii
children.description: A spectacular hummingbird with a tail that reaches 15 cm — longer than the rest of the body. Iridescent green body, blue throat, and extraordinary blue-violet tail streamers. Found in cloud forests of the Andes from Venezuela to Bolivia.

children.name: Loddigesia
children.description: The marvellous spatuletail — a monotypic genus and one of the most extraordinary hummingbirds in the world. The male has only four tail feathers, two of which are elongated bare shafts ending in broad, violet-blue discs or 'spatules' that move independently and are used in a mesmerising courtship dance.

children.name: Loddigesia mirabilis
children.description: One of the most remarkable birds on Earth. The male has just four tail feathers: the two outer ones are bare shafts tipped with iridescent violet-blue discs that buzz and swing during his courtship display. Endemic to a small area of the Peruvian Andes. Classified as Near Threatened due to habitat loss.

children.name: Amazilia
children.description: Emerald hummingbirds — a large genus of medium-sized hummingbirds found from Mexico through Central and South America. Most species are predominantly green, with vivid throat colours ranging from emerald to ruby. They are common garden visitors throughout their range.

children.name: Amazilia amazilia
children.description: Found in arid and semi-arid regions of coastal Peru and Ecuador. Green crown and back, white belly, and a distinctive rufous belly band. Adaptable to gardens and urban parks. Named after the Inca heroine Amazilia.

children.name: Amazilia tzacatl
children.description: One of the most common and widespread hummingbirds from Mexico to Ecuador. Bronze-green above, pale below, with a distinctive rufous tail that is constantly fanned. A regular visitor to gardens and feeders throughout its range.

children.name: Eulampis
children.description: Carib hummingbirds — two species endemic to the Caribbean islands. They are distinguished by brilliant iridescent throats and a highly curved bill adapted for feeding on heliconias and other tropical flowers. Important pollinators of Caribbean island ecosystems.

children.name: Eulampis jugularis
children.description: A large, dark hummingbird endemic to the Lesser Antilles. Males are iridescent purple and green with a brilliant amethyst throat. The bill is strongly curved, specialised for feeding on heliconia flowers. The female uses her longer bill to access deeper nectar sources.
